site.title: Trier Amphitheater
site.description: Complete guide to the Trier Amphitheater: 2nd-century Roman arena, UNESCO World Heritage Site, and testimony to ancient gladiatorial culture.
site.disclaimer: This site is an independent third-party tourist information portal. Information is sourced from public resources (tourism offices and Wikipedia) and Google Maps.

hero.title: Trier Amphitheater
hero.subtitle: 2nd-century Roman amphitheater showcasing ancient gladiatorial heritage

about.title: Discover the Amphitheater
about.description: The Trier Amphitheater, built around 100 AD, once served as the arena for gladiatorial combat and animal hunts in Roman Augusta Treverorum. With a capacity of up to 20,000 spectators, it is one of the best-preserved Roman amphitheaters north of the Alps and has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1986.
highlights: Panoramic views over Trier from upper tiers
Part of the Roman Monuments in Trier

items.title: Roman Architecture
items.text: Admire the impressive elliptical arena with its massive stone walls. The construction showcases Roman engineering mastery — the arena measures 75 × 50 meters and used the natural terrain of the Petrisberg as its foundation.

items.title: Underground Cellars
items.text: Descend into the well-preserved cellar vaults beneath the arena. Gladiators and wild animals were held here before the fights. The mechanisms for raising the cages are still recognizable.

items.title: Arena & Spectator Tiers
items.text: Step onto the arena floor and feel the atmosphere of ancient combat games. From the preserved spectator tiers, enjoy an impressive view over the entire arena and the city of Trier.

items.title: UNESCO World Heritage
items.text: As part of the 'Roman Monuments, Cathedral and Church of Our Lady in Trier,' the amphitheater was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1986. Together with the Porta Nigra and Imperial Baths, it forms a unique ensemble.

gallery.title: Photo Gallery
gallery.subtitle: Discover the amphitheater through our comprehensive photo collection

reviews.title: Real Visitor Reviews
reviews.declaration: Ratings and reviews are from Google Maps (last updated: 2026). We only show a selection of verified high-rated reviews. To see all complete and latest reviews, please click the link below.
reviews.viewAll: View all reviews on Google Maps

items.name: Thomas M.
items.text: Impressive Roman ruins! The underground cellars are fascinating. You can really imagine the gladiator fights. Absolutely worth seeing.

items.name: Sabine K.
items.text: A wonderful place full of history. The view from the upper tiers over Trier is breathtaking. Very well preserved for its age.

items.name: Markus W.
items.text: Rightly a UNESCO World Heritage Site! The atmosphere in the arena is unique. The audio guide tour is highly recommended.

items.name: Petra L.
items.text: Fantastic trip back to Roman times. The cellars beneath the arena are the highlight. Also perfect for families with children.

items.name: Jürgen B.
items.text: One of Germany's best-preserved amphitheaters. The size of the arena is impressive. Great combination with the Porta Nigra.

items.name: Claudia R.
items.text: Beautifully situated on the Petrisberg. Roman history comes alive here. The admission price is very fair.

items.name: Andreas H.
items.text: A must for every visit to Trier! The arena is larger than expected. The underground passages are especially impressive.

items.name: Eva S.
items.text: Great historical site with lots of atmosphere. Best visited early in the morning when it's still quiet.

hours.label: Opening Hours
hours.value: Open 9 AM – 4 PM — Seasonal changes possible. In summer (April–September) often open until 6 PM.

gettingThere.label: Getting There
gettingThere.value: From Trier Central Station: 20 min walk or 10 min by bus (Line 12). By car: A602, exit Trier-Olewig.

parking.label: Parking
parking.value: Free parking available directly at the amphitheater. Can be crowded in peak season — arriving early is recommended.

accessibility.label: Accessibility
accessibility.value: The upper area of the arena is accessible. Underground cellars have steep stairs — sturdy footwear recommended.

history.title: History & Culture
history.subtitle: From gladiatorial combat to UNESCO World Heritage

sections.heading: Construction & Architecture
sections.text: Built in the 2nd century under Emperor Antoninus Pius, the amphitheater cleverly exploits the natural slope of the Petrisberg — half of the spectator tiers rest directly on the rock, saving enormous amounts of building material. The elliptical arena measures 145 x 120 meters and could accommodate around 20,000 spectators. It simultaneously served as the eastern city gate of Augusta Treverorum's fortification wall.

sections.heading: Expansion under Constantius Chlorus
sections.text: When Emperor Constantius Chlorus relocated his residence to Trier in 293 AD, the amphitheater underwent extensive renovation. The underground chambers (hypogeum) were expanded — here gladiators, wild beasts, and condemned prisoners awaited their turn in the arena. Elevator mechanisms lifted them directly onto the arena floor.

sections.heading: Ancient Events
sections.text: The amphitheater was the social entertainment center of the Roman provincial capital. Besides gladiatorial combat, it hosted beast hunts (venationes), public executions, and political assemblies. The arena was the beating heart of social life in one of the most important cities of the Roman Empire.

sections.heading: Middle Ages & Rediscovery
sections.text: During the Middle Ages, the amphitheater served as a quarry — many buildings in Trier contain reused Roman stones. It was only from the 19th century that its historical significance was recognized and systematic excavations began. In 1986, it was declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site together with other Roman monuments in Trier.

itinerary.title: Professional Travel Routes
itinerary.subtitle: Three carefully designed Trier routes for different needs — all connecting core sites without backtracking

couple.title: Couple 2-Day Tour (Romantic + In-Depth)
couple.subtitle: Perfect for slow exploration, photography, and romantic wine tasting

days.label: Day 1: Old Town Roman Ruins + Riverside Romance

stops.name: Porta Nigra
stops.desc: Climb to the top for panoramic views of the old town, capturing Roman stone walls against red rooftops.

stops.name: Cathedral & Church of Our Lady
stops.desc: Germany's oldest cathedral with Holy Robe relics and Gothic domes. Perfect for romantic walks through the cloisters.

stops.name: Basilica of Constantine
stops.desc: Ancient Rome's largest pillarless hall with stunning light effects. Ideal for quiet couple photos.

stops.name: Imperial Baths (with lunch)
stops.desc: Explore underground heating tunnels, then relax at the on-site café.

stops.name: Hauptmarkt Square
stops.desc: Colorful medieval buildings and fountains. Buy flowers, enjoy coffee, and stroll the streets.

stops.name: Karl Marx House (optional)
stops.desc: Cultural stop to see 19th-century home and manuscripts.

stops.name: Roman Bridge + Riverside Walk
stops.desc: Ancient Roman stone bridge perfect for sunset silhouettes. Walk along the river to the wine district.

stops.name: Dinner: Moselle Riverside Restaurant
stops.desc: German pork knuckle with Moselle Riesling in candlelight.

days.label: Day 2: Amphitheater + Wine Country + Leisurely Time

stops.name: Trier Amphitheater
stops.desc: Explore underground dungeons and hillside seating with panoramic views. Fewer crowds, perfect photos.

stops.name: Barbara Baths
stops.desc: Lesser-known ruins with complete underground structures, perfect for quiet photography.

stops.name: Lunch: Old Town German Restaurant
stops.desc: Enjoy local specialties to recharge for the afternoon.

stops.name: Moselle Valley Wine Tasting
stops.desc: Take bus/taxi to nearby vineyards for wine tasting and romantic vineyard walks.

stops.name: Evening: Old Town Alleys + Dessert Shop
stops.desc: Buy chocolates/cakes and relax at a corner café.

family.title: Family 1-Day Tour (Easy + Educational + Interactive)
family.subtitle: Pace: Walking + Bus, rest every 1.5 hours, balancing history and fun for kids

stops.name: Porta Nigra
stops.desc: Tell Roman soldier stories, climb stairs to see wall structure. Kids love the height!

stops.name: Cathedral
stops.desc: See colorful glass windows and ancient stone carvings. Simple religious stories for children.

stops.name: Basilica of Constantine
stops.desc: Feel the 'huge room' and play echo games.

stops.name: Imperial Baths (with lunch)
stops.desc: Underground tunnel adventure, simulate ancient Roman bathing. On-site lunch available.

stops.name: Trier Amphitheater
stops.desc: Watch gladiator shows (summer), run on seating, find dungeon entrances. Highly interactive!

stops.name: Roman Bridge + Riverside Park
stops.desc: Feed pigeons, splash in water, have a picnic. Perfect for relaxing.

stops.name: Karl Marx House/State Museum (optional)
stops.desc: Museum has interactive children's exhibits and Roman artifact models.

stops.name: Dinner: Family-friendly Restaurant at Market Square
stops.desc: Choose restaurants with outdoor seating and kids' menus.

halfday.title: Family Half-Day Tour (Efficient + Classic, 3.5-4 hours)
halfday.subtitle: Route: City center loop, all walking, covers 4 UNESCO World Heritage sites

stops.name: Porta Nigra
stops.desc: Quick climb to the top for landmark photos.

stops.name: Cathedral & Church of Our Lady
stops.desc: Main hall + Holy Robe exhibition, concise visit.

stops.name: Basilica of Constantine
stops.desc: Quick exterior + interior tour to feel the space grandeur.

stops.name: Imperial Baths
stops.desc: See main baths and underground structures without deep exploration.

stops.name: Lunch/Quick Bite at Market Square
stops.desc: Square bakery and snack stands for quick refueling.

stops.name: Roman Bridge Viewing (optional)
stops.desc: 10-minute walk to see the ancient Roman stone bridge before returning.

generalTips.title: General Tips (Apply to All Routes)
tips: Tickets: Buy the 'Trier Roman Heritage Pass (Roman Pass)' first — covers all sites and saves 30%+ over individual tickets.
Transportation: Walk in city center; take bus 10-15 min to amphitheater/wineries. Day ticket ~5 euros.
Dining: Many choices at Market Square and Moselle riverside. Family tours prioritize restaurants with outdoor seating and kids' menus.
Photography: Best light before 9 AM and after 4 PM — perfect for Roman ruins and old town panoramas.
Season: Summer (May-September) has Roman-themed night tours and open-air performances — book in advance.
Comfort: Wear comfortable walking shoes, bring water and sunscreen; underground sites are cooler — bring a light jacket.

cards.title: Getting to the Amphitheater
cards.subtitle: Parking & Public Transport
points: Bus Line 12 from Central Station, stop 'Amphitheater' (10 min)
Free parking directly at the amphitheater (Olewiger Str.)
In peak season (May–October) arrive early — parking is limited
Rental bikes (nextbike) available at Central Station and Hauptmarkt
Walking from city center approx. 20–25 minutes (uphill, but scenic)

cards.title: Hotels Near the Roman Monuments
cards.subtitle: Recommended Accommodation
points: Hotel Deutscher Hof — Right at Porta Nigra, historic building, upscale
Ibis Styles Trier — Modern, budget-friendly, 10 min to Cathedral
Hotel Villa Hügel — Quiet location on Petrisberg, near Amphitheater
Youth Hostel Trier — Affordable, 15 min walk to city center
Holiday apartments at Hauptmarkt — Central, perfect for families

cards.title: 1-Day Trier: The Perfect Route
cards.subtitle: Amphitheater, Porta Nigra & Imperial Baths
points: Start 8:30 AM at Porta Nigra — least crowded in the morning
Cathedral + Basilica in the morning (all within walking distance)
Imperial Baths at lunchtime, snacks available on site
Roman Bridge at sunset as the perfect finale
